name: Acinic cell carcinoma
potential_causes: Largely sporadic with no consistently established cause; prior ionizing radiation to the head and neck is a recognized minor risk factor for salivary gland carcinomas.

name: Adenoid cystic carcinoma
potential_causes: Cause not established; salivary and other adenoid cystic carcinomas are largely sporadic, with prior ionizing radiation to the head and neck the only recognized minor risk factor.

name: Basal cell adenocarcinoma
potential_causes: Cause not established; usually sporadic, though rare cases arise in patients with membranous basal cell adenoma and hereditary cylindromatosis (CYLD-related), otherwise no known cause.
source: WHO Classification of Head and Neck Tumours, 5th ed. (2022)

name: Carcinoma ex pleomorphic adenoma
potential_causes: Arises from malignant transformation of a preexisting benign pleomorphic adenoma, so a long-standing or recurrent pleomorphic adenoma is the established precursor lesion; prior radiation is a minor risk factor.
source: WHO Classification of Head and Neck Tumours, 5th ed. (2022)

name: Ceruminous adenocarcinoma
aliases: ceruminous gland carcinoma of external auditory canal
potential_causes: Cause not established; a rare sporadic adenocarcinoma of the ceruminous glands of the external auditory canal with no known environmental or inherited cause.
source: WHO Classification of Head and Neck Tumours, 5th ed. (2022)

name: Clear cell carcinoma
potential_causes: Cause not established; this salivary gland carcinoma (EWSR1::ATF1 fusion) is sporadic with no known environmental or inherited etiologic factor.
source: WHO Classification of Head and Neck Tumours, 5th ed. (2022)

name: Endolymphatic sac tumor
aliases: Heffner tumor; low-grade adenocarcinoma of endolymphatic sac
potential_causes: Frequently caused by von Hippel-Lindau disease, an inherited VHL tumor suppressor syndrome; up to about 16 percent of VHL patients develop these tumors through biallelic VHL inactivation, while other cases are sporadic.
source: WHO Classification of Head and Neck Tumours, 5th ed. (2022)

name: Epithelial-myoepithelial carcinoma
potential_causes: Cause not established; a rare sporadic salivary gland carcinoma with no proven environmental or inherited cause, though prior head and neck radiation may be a minor risk factor.
source: WHO Classification of Head and Neck Tumours, 5th ed. (2022)

name: Fetal adenocarcinoma of the lung
aliases: well-differentiated fetal adenocarcinoma; pulmonary endodermal tumor resembling fetal lung
potential_causes: Cause not established; a rare lung adenocarcinoma variant that is largely sporadic (well-differentiated form has recurrent CTNNB1 mutations), though smoking has been reported in the high-grade form.
source: WHO Classification of Thoracic Tumours, 5th ed. (2021)

name: HPV-related multiphenotypic sinonasal carcinoma
aliases: HMSC; HPV-related carcinoma with adenoid cystic-like features
potential_causes: Caused by high-risk human papillomavirus infection (most often HPV type 33, also 16, 31, 35, 52, 56), which is consistently present and drives this sinonasal carcinoma.
source: WHO Classification of Head and Neck Tumours, 5th ed. (2022)

name: Intestinal-type sinonasal adenocarcinoma
potential_causes: Occupational exposure to hardwood dust and leather (tanning) dust is the established cause, with these tumors classically arising in woodworkers and leather workers after long latency.
source: WHO Classification of Head and Neck Tumours, 5th ed. (2022)

name: Intraductal carcinoma
potential_causes: Cause not established; a rare sporadic salivary gland carcinoma with no known environmental, infectious, or inherited cause.
source: WHO Classification of Head and Neck Tumours, 5th ed. (2022)

name: Large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma of the lung
potential_causes: Tobacco smoking is the established main cause, as for other high-grade pulmonary neuroendocrine carcinomas.
source: WHO Classification of Thoracic Tumours, 5th ed. (2021)

name: Laryngeal neuroendocrine carcinoma
aliases: laryngeal small cell carcinoma; laryngeal large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma
potential_causes: Tobacco smoking is a recognized risk factor for laryngeal neuroendocrine carcinomas, consistent with other smoking-related aerodigestive neuroendocrine tumors; otherwise many cases have no established cause.
source: WHO Classification of Head and Neck Tumours, 5th ed. (2022)

name: Lymphoepithelial carcinoma
potential_causes: Strongly associated with Epstein-Barr virus, particularly in endemic populations (for example southern Chinese and Inuit), where the virus is present in a clonal form within the tumor cells and is implicated in oncogenesis.
source: WHO Classification of Head and Neck Tumours, 5th ed. (2022)

name: Mesothelioma of the tunica vaginalis
potential_causes: Asbestos exposure is implicated in a subset, and prior scrotal trauma, hydrocele, or hernia have been proposed as predisposing conditions, but many cases have no established cause.
source: WHO Classification of Urinary and Male Genital Tumours, 5th ed. (2022)

name: Metaplastic thymoma
potential_causes: Cause not established; a rare sporadic biphasic thymoma (recurrent YAP1::MAML2 fusion) with no known environmental or inherited cause.
source: WHO Classification of Thoracic Tumours, 5th ed. (2021)

name: Micronodular thymoma with lymphoid stroma
potential_causes: Cause not established; a rare sporadic thymoma variant with no known environmental or inherited cause.
source: WHO Classification of Thoracic Tumours, 5th ed. (2021)

name: Microsecretory adenocarcinoma
potential_causes: Cause not established; a recently described rare sporadic salivary gland carcinoma (MEF2C::SS18 fusion) with no known environmental or inherited cause.
source: WHO Classification of Head and Neck Tumours, 5th ed. (2022)

name: Middle ear adenocarcinoma
aliases: aggressive papillary tumor of middle ear (obsolete)
potential_causes: Cause not established; a very rare sporadic tumor of the middle ear with no known environmental, infectious, or inherited cause.
source: WHO Classification of Head and Neck Tumours, 5th ed. (2022)

name: Middle ear squamous cell carcinoma
aliases: temporal bone squamous cell carcinoma; external auditory canal squamous cell carcinoma
potential_causes: Chronic long-standing suppurative otitis media and inflammation are the main recognized predisposing conditions; prior radiation to the region is an additional risk factor.
source: WHO Classification of Head and Neck Tumours, 5th ed. (2022)

name: Mucinous adenocarcinoma
potential_causes: Cause not established; salivary mucinous adenocarcinoma is a rare sporadic tumor (recurrent AKT1 mutations described) with no known environmental or inherited etiologic factor.
source: WHO Classification of Head and Neck Tumours, 5th ed. (2022)

name: Mucoepidermoid carcinoma
potential_causes: Mostly sporadic; prior ionizing radiation exposure to the head and neck (for example therapeutic radiotherapy or atomic bomb survivors) is the main established risk factor for this salivary gland carcinoma.

name: Myoepithelial carcinoma
potential_causes: Cause not established; sporadic, arising de novo or from a preexisting pleomorphic adenoma or myoepithelioma, with no known environmental or inherited cause.
source: WHO Classification of Head and Neck Tumours, 5th ed. (2022)

name: Nasopharyngeal carcinoma
aliases: NPC; keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma of nasopharynx; nonkeratinizing nasopharyngeal carcinoma; basaloid squamous cell carcinoma of nasopharynx
potential_causes: Epstein-Barr virus infection is the central causal agent, acting together with dietary salt-preserved fish, tobacco smoking, inherited HLA class I susceptibility, and family history, which together produce its distinctive geographic distribution.

name: Non-intestinal-type sinonasal adenocarcinoma
potential_causes: Cause largely not established; most cases are sporadic, though some sinonasal adenocarcinomas are linked to wood dust and other occupational inhalant exposures.
source: WHO Classification of Head and Neck Tumours, 5th ed. (2022)

name: NUT carcinoma
aliases: NUT midline carcinoma; nuclear protein in testis carcinoma
potential_causes: Cause not established; NUT carcinoma arises from a sporadic somatic NUTM1 rearrangement (usually BRD4::NUTM1) with no known environmental, lifestyle, or inherited risk factor.

name: Pericardial mesothelioma
potential_causes: Largely sporadic and its link to asbestos is weaker than for pleural mesothelioma; asbestos exposure and prior radiation are possible risk factors but a cause is often not established in individual cases.
source: WHO Classification of Thoracic Tumours, 5th ed. (2021)

name: Peritoneal mesothelioma
potential_causes: Asbestos exposure is the principal established cause; erionite exposure and prior therapeutic radiation are additional recognized risk factors, and germline BAP1 mutation predisposes to mesothelioma.

name: Pleuropulmonary blastoma
potential_causes: Frequently caused by an inherited DICER1 germline mutation (DICER1 tumor predisposition syndrome, autosomal dominant), with a second somatic DICER1 hit; a subset is sporadic.

name: Polymorphous adenocarcinoma
potential_causes: Cause not established; this minor salivary gland carcinoma is sporadic with no known environmental, infectious, or inherited etiology.
source: WHO Classification of Head and Neck Tumours, 5th ed. (2022)

name: Pulmonary adenoid cystic carcinoma
aliases: salivary gland-type adenoid cystic carcinoma of lung
potential_causes: Cause not established; this tracheobronchial salivary-type carcinoma is sporadic and not clearly related to smoking, with no known environmental or inherited cause.
source: WHO Classification of Thoracic Tumours, 5th ed. (2021)

name: Pulmonary atypical carcinoid
aliases: atypical carcinoid tumor of lung; intermediate-grade pulmonary neuroendocrine tumor
potential_causes: Largely sporadic; a minority are associated with inherited MEN1 syndrome, and unlike typical carcinoid there is some association with tobacco smoking.
source: WHO Classification of Thoracic Tumours, 5th ed. (2021)

name: Pulmonary blastoma
potential_causes: Cause not established; a rare biphasic lung tumor that is largely sporadic, though tobacco smoking has been reported in some patients, with no proven inherited cause.
source: WHO Classification of Thoracic Tumours, 5th ed. (2021)

name: Pulmonary carcinosarcoma
potential_causes: Tobacco smoking is the established main risk factor, consistent with its biphasic non-small cell lung carcinoma nature.
source: WHO Classification of Thoracic Tumours, 5th ed. (2021)

name: Pulmonary epithelial-myoepithelial carcinoma
aliases: salivary gland-type epithelial-myoepithelial carcinoma of lung
potential_causes: Cause not established; a very rare sporadic bronchial salivary-type carcinoma with no known environmental or inherited cause.
source: WHO Classification of Thoracic Tumours, 5th ed. (2021)

name: Pulmonary hyalinizing clear cell carcinoma
aliases: salivary gland-type hyalinizing clear cell carcinoma of lung
potential_causes: Cause not established; a rare sporadic salivary-type lung carcinoma (EWSR1::ATF1 fusion) with no known environmental or inherited cause.
source: WHO Classification of Thoracic Tumours, 5th ed. (2021)

name: Pulmonary lymphoepithelial carcinoma
potential_causes: Strongly associated with Epstein-Barr virus in Asian patients, where over 90 percent of cases carry EBV within the tumor cells; in Western populations the EBV association is much weaker.
source: WHO Classification of Thoracic Tumours, 5th ed. (2021)

name: Pulmonary mucoepidermoid carcinoma
aliases: salivary gland-type mucoepidermoid carcinoma of lung
potential_causes: Cause not established; this bronchial salivary-type carcinoma is sporadic and not clearly linked to smoking, with no known inherited or environmental cause.
source: WHO Classification of Thoracic Tumours, 5th ed. (2021)

name: Pulmonary NUT carcinoma
potential_causes: Cause not established; arises from a sporadic somatic NUTM1 rearrangement with no known environmental, lifestyle, or inherited risk factor and no consistent link to smoking.
source: WHO Classification of Thoracic Tumours, 5th ed. (2021)

name: Pulmonary sarcomatoid carcinoma
aliases: pleomorphic carcinoma of lung; spindle cell carcinoma of lung; giant cell carcinoma of lung
potential_causes: Tobacco smoking is the established main risk factor, as for other non-small cell lung carcinomas; asbestos exposure has also been reported.
source: WHO Classification of Thoracic Tumours, 5th ed. (2021)

name: Pulmonary typical carcinoid
aliases: typical carcinoid tumor of lung; low-grade pulmonary neuroendocrine tumor
potential_causes: Largely sporadic and not clearly related to smoking; a minority arise in inherited multiple endocrine neoplasia type 1 (MEN1), an established predisposing syndrome, or in diffuse idiopathic pulmonary neuroendocrine cell hyperplasia.
source: WHO Classification of Thoracic Tumours, 5th ed. (2021)

name: Respiratory epithelial adenomatoid hamartoma-associated adenocarcinoma
potential_causes: Arises in association with a preexisting respiratory epithelial adenomatoid hamartoma, which serves as a precursor lesion; the underlying cause of the hamartoma itself is not established.
source: WHO Classification of Head and Neck Tumours, 5th ed. (2022)

name: Salivary carcinosarcoma
aliases: carcinosarcoma ex pleomorphic adenoma; true malignant mixed tumor
potential_causes: Cause not established; usually arises from a preexisting pleomorphic adenoma (true malignant mixed tumor) but is otherwise sporadic with no known environmental or inherited cause.
source: WHO Classification of Head and Neck Tumours, 5th ed. (2022)

name: Salivary duct carcinoma
potential_causes: Cause not established; largely sporadic, arising de novo or from carcinoma ex pleomorphic adenoma, with prior head and neck radiation a possible minor risk factor.
source: WHO Classification of Head and Neck Tumours, 5th ed. (2022)

name: Sclerosing microcystic adenocarcinoma
potential_causes: Cause not established; a very rare sporadic salivary gland carcinoma with no known environmental or inherited etiology.
source: WHO Classification of Head and Neck Tumours, 5th ed. (2022)

name: Secretory carcinoma
potential_causes: Cause not established; this salivary and skin tumor is sporadic and defined by an ETV6::NTRK3 fusion, with no known environmental or inherited etiologic factor.
source: WHO Classification of Head and Neck Tumours, 5th ed. (2022)

name: Sialoblastoma
potential_causes: Cause not established; a rare congenital or perinatal salivary gland tumor of presumed embryonal origin, sporadic, with no proven inherited or environmental cause.
source: WHO Classification of Head and Neck Tumours, 5th ed. (2022)

name: Sinonasal neuroendocrine carcinoma
aliases: sinonasal small cell carcinoma; sinonasal large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma
potential_causes: Cause not established; a rare sporadic sinonasal carcinoma with no proven environmental, infectious, or inherited etiologic factor.
source: WHO Classification of Head and Neck Tumours, 5th ed. (2022)

name: Sinonasal teratocarcinosarcoma
potential_causes: Cause not established; a rare aggressive sinonasal tumor of mixed epithelial, mesenchymal, and neuroepithelial elements that is sporadic with no known environmental or inherited cause.
source: WHO Classification of Head and Neck Tumours, 5th ed. (2022)

name: Sinonasal undifferentiated carcinoma
potential_causes: Cause largely not established; most cases are sporadic, though occupational exposures such as wood dust and nickel are implicated in sinonasal cancers broadly and a subset is IDH2-mutant.

name: SMARCA4-deficient sinonasal carcinoma
potential_causes: Cause not established; a sporadic sinonasal carcinoma defined by somatic SMARCA4 (BRG1) loss, with no known environmental or inherited etiologic factor.
source: WHO Classification of Head and Neck Tumours, 5th ed. (2022)

name: SMARCB1-deficient sinonasal carcinoma
potential_causes: Cause not established; a sporadic sinonasal carcinoma defined by somatic biallelic SMARCB1 (INI1) inactivation, with no known environmental, infectious, or inherited etiologic factor.
source: WHO Classification of Head and Neck Tumours, 5th ed. (2022)

name: Spindle cell squamous cell carcinoma of the larynx
aliases: sarcomatoid carcinoma of larynx; spindle cell carcinoma
potential_causes: Tobacco smoking and alcohol use are the established risk factors, shared with conventional laryngeal squamous cell carcinoma; prior radiation is an additional risk factor.
source: WHO Classification of Head and Neck Tumours, 5th ed. (2022)

name: Squamous cell carcinoma of salivary gland
potential_causes: Primary salivary squamous cell carcinoma is rare and largely sporadic; prior head and neck radiation is a recognized risk factor, and metastasis or extension of cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma must be excluded.
source: WHO Classification of Head and Neck Tumours, 5th ed. (2022)

name: Thymic carcinoma
aliases: thymic squamous cell carcinoma; type C thymoma (obsolete)
potential_causes: Cause not established; thymic carcinoma is sporadic with no proven environmental, infectious, or inherited etiology, although a minority of EBV-associated lymphoepithelial subtypes occur.

name: Thymic neuroendocrine tumor
aliases: thymic carcinoid; thymic atypical carcinoid; thymic neuroendocrine carcinoma
potential_causes: Mostly sporadic; a substantial minority arise in the setting of inherited multiple endocrine neoplasia type 1 (MEN1), which is an established predisposing syndrome, and there is an association with tobacco smoking.
source: WHO Classification of Thoracic Tumours, 5th ed. (2021)

name: Thymoma
potential_causes: Cause not established; most thymomas are sporadic with no known environmental or inherited cause. They are strongly associated with autoimmune paraneoplastic conditions, most notably myasthenia gravis, but that association is not itself an established cause.

name: Thymoma type AB
potential_causes: Cause not established; like other thymomas it is sporadic with no known environmental or inherited cause, though it may associate with paraneoplastic autoimmune disease.
source: WHO Classification of Thoracic Tumours, 5th ed. (2021)

name: Thymoma type B1
potential_causes: Cause not established; sporadic with no known environmental or inherited cause, commonly associated with myasthenia gravis as a paraneoplastic association rather than a cause.
source: WHO Classification of Thoracic Tumours, 5th ed. (2021)

name: Thymoma type B2
potential_causes: Cause not established; sporadic with no known environmental or inherited cause, frequently associated with myasthenia gravis as an association not a cause.
source: WHO Classification of Thoracic Tumours, 5th ed. (2021)

name: Thymoma type B3
potential_causes: Cause not established; sporadic with no known environmental or inherited cause, often associated with myasthenia gravis as a paraneoplastic association.
source: WHO Classification of Thoracic Tumours, 5th ed. (2021)

name: Tracheal adenoid cystic carcinoma
potential_causes: Cause not established; this tracheal salivary-type carcinoma is sporadic and not related to smoking, with no known environmental or inherited cause.
source: WHO Classification of Thoracic Tumours, 5th ed. (2021)

name: Tracheal squamous cell carcinoma
potential_causes: Tobacco smoking is the established main risk factor, shared with other aerodigestive squamous cell carcinomas.
source: WHO Classification of Thoracic Tumours, 5th ed. (2021)

name: Well-differentiated papillary mesothelial tumor
potential_causes: Cause not established; most are sporadic with an uncertain and generally weak relationship to asbestos, and no proven inherited cause.
source: WHO Classification of Thoracic Tumours, 5th ed. (2021)

name: Acinar cell carcinoma of the pancreas
potential_causes: Predominantly sporadic with no established cause; weak associations include tobacco smoking and rare occurrence within familial cancer syndromes (familial adenomatous polyposis, Lynch syndrome, Peutz-Jeghers), but for most cases the etiology is not established.

name: Ampullary carcinoma
aliases: Cancer of the ampulla of Vater; Carcinoma of the ampulla of Vater; Ampulla of Vater adenocarcinoma
potential_causes: Most cases are sporadic and arise from precursor adenomas of the ampulla of Vater; the strongest predisposing condition is familial adenomatous polyposis (germline APC), which greatly increases risk of duodenal and ampullary adenomas and carcinoma, with Lynch syndrome as a lesser hereditary contributor.

name: Anal melanoma
aliases: Anorectal melanoma; Mucosal melanoma of the anus
potential_causes: Rare mucosal melanoma of the anorectum; unlike cutaneous melanoma it is not linked to ultraviolet exposure, and no established environmental or hereditary cause has been identified, so the etiology is not established.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System; NCI

name: Appendiceal goblet cell adenocarcinoma
potential_causes: Rare appendiceal tumor with mixed goblet cell and adenocarcinomatous features; it is essentially sporadic with no established causal exposure or hereditary syndrome, so the etiology is not established.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System

name: Appendiceal mucinous adenocarcinoma
potential_causes: Sporadic invasive mucinous adenocarcinoma of the appendix, often KRAS/GNAS mutated; no established carcinogen, infection, or hereditary cause, so the etiology is not established.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System

name: Appendiceal neuroendocrine tumor
potential_causes: Usually an incidental sporadic tumor of the appendiceal tip; no established environmental cause and no consistent hereditary predisposition, so the etiology is not established.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System

name: BRAF-mutant gastrointestinal stromal tumor
potential_causes: A KIT/PDGFRA wild-type GIST driven by a somatic BRAF V600E mutation; sporadic with no established external or hereditary cause, and the mutation is a driver rather than an etiologic factor.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System

name: Colorectal mucinous adenocarcinoma
aliases: Mucinous adenocarcinoma of the colon and rectum
potential_causes: A colorectal adenocarcinoma subtype with abundant extracellular mucin; shares standard colorectal cancer risk factors and is enriched for microsatellite instability, so it is associated with mismatch repair deficiency and Lynch syndrome in addition to the general risk profile (inflammatory bowel disease, precursor adenomas).
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System

name: Colorectal neuroendocrine tumor
aliases: Colonic NET; Rectal NET; Rectal carcinoid
potential_causes: Well-differentiated neuroendocrine tumor of colon or rectum; usually sporadic and often found incidentally, with no established external cause or consistent hereditary predisposition, so the etiology is not established.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System

name: Combined hepatocellular-cholangiocarcinoma
potential_causes: A biphenotypic primary liver cancer that arises on the same backgrounds as hepatocellular carcinoma, notably cirrhosis from chronic hepatitis B or C infection, alcohol-related liver disease, and other chronic liver injury.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System

name: Distal cholangiocarcinoma
aliases: dCCA; Distal bile duct carcinoma; Extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma
potential_causes: Shares biliary risk factors with other cholangiocarcinomas: primary sclerosing cholangitis, choledochal cysts, hepatolithiasis, chronic biliary inflammation, and liver fluke infection (Opisthorchis viverrini, Clonorchis sinensis) in endemic areas.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System

name: Duodenal gangliocytic paraganglioma
potential_causes: Rare tumor of the periampullary duodenum of uncertain histogenesis; overwhelmingly sporadic with no established cause, though a minority occur in patients with neurofibromatosis type 1, and for most cases the etiology is not established.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System

name: Esophageal adenoid cystic carcinoma
aliases: Adenoid cystic carcinoma of the esophagus
potential_causes: Extremely rare tumor arising from esophageal submucosal glands; no established risk factor, carcinogen, or predisposing condition has been identified and the cause is genuinely unknown.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System

name: Esophageal small cell neuroendocrine carcinoma
aliases: Small cell carcinoma of the esophagus; Esophageal SCNEC
potential_causes: Rare and aggressive; shares risk factors with esophageal squamous cell carcinoma including tobacco smoking and heavy alcohol use, though the specific etiology of the neuroendocrine phenotype is not well established.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System

name: Esophageal undifferentiated carcinoma
potential_causes: A rare high-grade esophageal carcinoma; it is presumed to share the major esophageal carcinogenic exposures (tobacco, alcohol for squamous-type; reflux/Barrett esophagus for glandular-type), but a specific established cause for the undifferentiated form is not defined.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System

name: Fibrolamellar hepatocellular carcinoma
potential_causes: Occurs in adolescents and young adults without cirrhosis or underlying liver disease; it is defined by a somatic DNAJB1-PRKACA fusion but has no established etiologic risk factor, virus, or predisposing exposure, so the cause is genuinely unknown.

name: Gallbladder carcinoma
aliases: Carcinoma of the gallbladder; Gallbladder adenocarcinoma
potential_causes: Strongly caused by chronic gallbladder inflammation, most often from gallstones (cholelithiasis) and chronic cholecystitis; other risk factors include porcelain gallbladder, gallbladder polyps, chronic Salmonella typhi carriage, and anomalous pancreaticobiliary junction.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System; RARECAREnet

name: Gastrinoma
aliases: Gastrin-producing neuroendocrine tumor; Zollinger-Ellison syndrome tumor
potential_causes: Gastrin-secreting neuroendocrine tumor of the pancreas or duodenum; roughly a quarter arise in multiple endocrine neoplasia type 1 (MEN1), which is an established hereditary cause, while the remaining sporadic tumors have no established external cause.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System; Orphanet

name: Gastroenteropancreatic neuroendocrine carcinoma
aliases: GEP-NEC; Poorly differentiated neuroendocrine carcinoma; Small cell neuroendocrine carcinoma; Large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma
potential_causes: High-grade neuroendocrine carcinoma of the GI tract or pancreas; predominantly sporadic with no established external cause, and unlike well-differentiated tumors it is rarely syndrome-associated, so the etiology is not established.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System

name: Gastrointestinal stromal tumor
potential_causes: Most are sporadic and driven by somatic KIT or PDGFRA mutations with no external cause, but predisposing conditions exist: neurofibromatosis type 1, Carney triad, Carney-Stratakis syndrome, and rare familial germline KIT/PDGFRA mutations all increase risk.

name: Glucagonoma
potential_causes: Rare glucagon-secreting pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor; usually sporadic with no established external cause, occasionally associated with multiple endocrine neoplasia type 1 (MEN1).
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System; Orphanet

name: Hepatoblastoma
potential_causes: The most common pediatric liver cancer, strongly associated with hereditary syndromes including Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome and familial adenomatous polyposis (germline APC), and with prematurity and very low birth weight; most sporadic cases have activating CTNNB1 (beta-catenin) mutations.

name: Hepatoid adenocarcinoma of the stomach
potential_causes: A rare alpha-fetoprotein producing gastric adenocarcinoma variant; it shares the general risk profile of gastric cancer (Helicobacter pylori infection, chronic atrophic gastritis) but no cause specific to the hepatoid phenotype is established.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System

name: High-grade appendiceal mucinous neoplasm
potential_causes: Sporadic high-grade mucinous neoplasm of the appendix; like its low-grade counterpart it has no established external cause or hereditary predisposition and the etiology is not established.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System

name: Insulinoma
aliases: Insulin-producing pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor; insulin-producing neuroendocrine tumor
potential_causes: Usually a sporadic pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor with no established external cause, but about 5 to 10 percent occur in multiple endocrine neoplasia type 1 (MEN1); the sporadic cases are considered idiopathic.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System; Orphanet

name: Intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasm with associated invasive carcinoma
potential_causes: Invasive carcinoma arising from an intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasm, a defined precursor lesion of the pancreatic ducts; IPMNs are largely sporadic (KRAS/GNAS driven) but risk is increased in familial pancreatic cancer settings such as Peutz-Jeghers syndrome, with the precursor lesion itself being the established route to invasion.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System

name: Low-grade appendiceal mucinous neoplasm
potential_causes: Sporadic mucinous epithelial neoplasm of the appendix commonly harboring KRAS/GNAS mutations; no established environmental cause, infectious agent, or hereditary predisposition, so the etiology is not established.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System

name: Medullary carcinoma of the colon
potential_causes: Characteristically a microsatellite instability high tumor caused by mismatch repair deficiency, most often sporadic MLH1 promoter hypermethylation (frequently with BRAF V600E), and a minority due to inherited Lynch syndrome; the MMR-deficient hypermutated pathway is the established basis.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System

name: Mixed neuroendocrine-non-neuroendocrine neoplasm
potential_causes: A composite tumor combining neuroendocrine and non-neuroendocrine (usually adenocarcinoma) components; it is sporadic and thought to derive from a common precursor, with no established distinct etiologic cause.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System

name: Mucinous cystic neoplasm of the pancreas with associated invasive carcinoma
aliases: Invasive mucinous cystic neoplasm; MCN-associated carcinoma
potential_causes: Invasive carcinoma arising from a mucinous cystic neoplasm, a precursor lesion occurring almost exclusively in women and characterized by an ovarian-type stroma; the precursor cyst is the established route to invasion, but no external etiologic exposure is established for the neoplasm itself.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System

name: NF1-associated gastrointestinal stromal tumor
potential_causes: GIST arising in patients with neurofibromatosis type 1 due to germline NF1 loss; the inherited NF1 syndrome is the established cause, and these tumors are typically multiple and located in the small intestine.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System

name: Pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor
aliases: PanNET; Islet cell tumor; Nonfunctional pancreatic NET
potential_causes: Most are sporadic with no established environmental cause, but a substantial minority arise in hereditary syndromes: multiple endocrine neoplasia type 1 (MEN1), von Hippel-Lindau disease, neurofibromatosis type 1, and tuberous sclerosis all predispose to pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors.

name: Pancreatoblastoma
potential_causes: Rare embryonal pancreatic tumor of childhood associated with Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome and with familial adenomatous polyposis (APC/Wnt pathway alterations); the majority of sporadic cases have no established external cause.

name: PDGFRA-mutant gastrointestinal stromal tumor
potential_causes: A molecular subtype of GIST driven by a somatic activating PDGFRA mutation (commonly D842V); it is sporadic with no established external or hereditary cause, and the mutation is a driver rather than an etiologic exposure.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System

name: Perianal Paget disease
aliases: Anal Paget disease; Extramammary Paget disease of the anus
potential_causes: Intraepithelial adenocarcinoma of perianal skin; a substantial proportion is secondary to an underlying anorectal or other visceral adenocarcinoma spreading to the epidermis, while primary cases arising from apocrine gland cells have no established external cause.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System

name: Perihilar cholangiocarcinoma
potential_causes: Established risk factors include primary sclerosing cholangitis, chronic biliary inflammation, choledochal cysts and Caroli disease, hepatolithiasis, and in endemic regions liver fluke infection (Opisthorchis viverrini, Clonorchis sinensis); chronic biliary tract inflammation is the unifying mechanism.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System

name: Pseudomyxoma peritonei
potential_causes: Nearly always caused by rupture of a low-grade appendiceal mucinous neoplasm (less often a mucinous tumor of ovary, colon, or other sites) that seeds the peritoneum with mucin-producing epithelium; the underlying appendiceal neoplasm itself is sporadic with no established external cause.

name: Quadruple wild-type gastrointestinal stromal tumor
aliases: Quadruple WT GIST; KIT/PDGFRA/SDH/RAS wild-type GIST
potential_causes: GIST lacking KIT, PDGFRA, RAS-pathway, and SDH alterations, a heterogeneous molecular group; sporadic with no established external cause or defined predisposing condition, so the etiology is not established.
source: Pantaleo Cancer Med 2015 (PMID 25470732)

name: SDH-deficient gastrointestinal stromal tumor
potential_causes: Wild-type GIST subtype lacking KIT/PDGFRA mutations, caused by loss of succinate dehydrogenase function; often arises with germline SDHx mutations and occurs in Carney-Stratakis syndrome and (non-germline) Carney triad, making it a hereditary/syndromic tumor.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System; Boikos JAMA Oncol 2016 (PMID 27011036)

name: Serous cystadenocarcinoma of the pancreas
potential_causes: Exceedingly rare malignant counterpart of serous cystadenoma; benign serous cystic neoplasms are linked to von Hippel-Lindau disease, but for the very rare carcinoma there is no established cause, so the etiology is not established.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System

name: Signet ring cell carcinoma of the colon and rectum
potential_causes: A poorly differentiated colorectal adenocarcinoma variant; shares colorectal cancer risk factors (chronic inflammatory bowel disease, and it is enriched in Lynch syndrome and microsatellite-unstable tumors), though many cases are sporadic with the general colorectal risk profile.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System

name: Small intestinal adenocarcinoma
aliases: Adenocarcinoma of the small intestine; Duodenal adenocarcinoma; Jejunal adenocarcinoma; Ileal adenocarcinoma
potential_causes: Predisposing conditions are well established: Crohn disease and celiac disease (chronic small bowel inflammation), and hereditary syndromes familial adenomatous polyposis, Lynch syndrome, and Peutz-Jeghers syndrome; most tumors arise from precursor adenomas.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System; RARECAREnet

name: Solid pseudopapillary neoplasm of the pancreas
aliases: SPN; Frantz tumor; Solid pseudopapillary tumor
potential_causes: Low-grade pancreatic neoplasm overwhelmingly in young women, defined by somatic CTNNB1 (beta-catenin) mutations; despite hypotheses about female sex hormones, there is no established etiologic cause and it is considered idiopathic/sporadic.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System

name: Somatostatinoma
potential_causes: Rare somatostatin-secreting neuroendocrine tumor of the pancreas or duodenum; associations exist with neurofibromatosis type 1 (especially duodenal tumors) and multiple endocrine neoplasia type 1, but most pancreatic cases are sporadic with no established external cause.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System; Orphanet

name: Squamous cell carcinoma of the stomach
potential_causes: Extremely rare primary gastric squamous cell carcinoma; no established cause specific to this entity, and it is not linked to the usual gastric adenocarcinoma drivers, so the etiology is not established.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System

name: Type 1 gastric neuroendocrine tumor
aliases: ECL-cell NET type 1; Type 1 gastric carcinoid
potential_causes: Caused by autoimmune chronic atrophic gastritis: destruction of oxyntic mucosa raises gastrin, and chronic hypergastrinemia drives enterochromaffin-like cell hyperplasia and neoplasia; strongly linked to pernicious anemia and autoimmune metaplastic atrophic gastritis.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System

name: Type 2 gastric neuroendocrine tumor
aliases: ECL-cell NET type 2; Type 2 gastric carcinoid
potential_causes: Caused by hypergastrinemia from a gastrinoma in the setting of Zollinger-Ellison syndrome, almost always within multiple endocrine neoplasia type 1 (MEN1); excess gastrin drives enterochromaffin-like cell proliferation.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System

name: Type 3 gastric neuroendocrine tumor
aliases: Sporadic gastric NET; Type 3 gastric carcinoid
potential_causes: Sporadic gastrin-independent tumor arising on a background of normal gastric mucosa and normal serum gastrin; no chronic gastritis or hypergastrinemia driver and no established external cause, so the etiology is not established.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System

name: Undifferentiated carcinoma of the pancreas with osteoclast-like giant cells
aliases: Osteoclastic giant cell tumor of the pancreas
potential_causes: Rare pancreatic carcinoma variant harboring KRAS mutations similar to conventional ductal adenocarcinoma; considered part of the pancreatic ductal carcinoma spectrum but with no established distinct etiologic cause, so the etiology is not established.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System

name: VIPoma
aliases: Vasoactive intestinal peptide-secreting tumor; Verner-Morrison syndrome tumor; WDHA syndrome tumor
potential_causes: Rare vasoactive intestinal peptide secreting pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor; mostly sporadic with no established external cause, though a minority occur in multiple endocrine neoplasia type 1 (MEN1).
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System; Orphanet

name: Acquired cystic disease-associated renal cell carcinoma
potential_causes: Caused by end-stage renal disease with long-term dialysis and acquired cystic kidney disease, which is the defining predisposing condition; risk rises with dialysis duration.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; WHO 2022 classification of renal neoplasms Cureus

name: Adenocarcinoma of the rete testis
potential_causes: Cause not established; it is extremely rare and idiopathic, with rete testis adenomatous hyperplasia proposed as a precursor and only unproven associations with hydrocele, chronic inflammation, and chemical exposures.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; Pathology Outlines testis and paratestis WHO classification

name: Adult granulosa cell tumor of the ovary
potential_causes: No established external cause; nearly all harbor a somatic FOXL2 C134W mutation, which is a molecular hallmark rather than an etiologic exposure, so the underlying cause is not established.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; Cancer Research UK sex cord stromal ovarian tumours

name: ALK-rearranged renal cell carcinoma
potential_causes: Sporadic renal carcinoma driven by ALK gene fusions; some cases occur with sickle cell trait, but no consistent environmental, infectious, or clearly established hereditary cause is defined.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; WHO 2022 classification of renal neoplasms Cureus

name: Chromophobe renal cell carcinoma
potential_causes: Mostly sporadic with no established environmental cause; a fraction arises in Birt-Hogg-Dube syndrome (germline FLCN mutation), which predisposes to chromophobe and hybrid oncocytic tumors.

name: Clear cell adenocarcinoma of the cervix
potential_causes: A subset is caused by in utero diethylstilbestrol (DES) exposure, which produced cervicovaginal adenosis; most contemporary cases are HPV-independent and sporadic with no established cause.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; clear cell adenocarcinoma of the cervix PMC7243845

name: Clear cell adenocarcinoma of the urethra
aliases: Mullerian-type clear cell adenocarcinoma of the urethra
potential_causes: Rare urethral adenocarcinoma often thought to arise from urethral diverticulum or nephrogenic adenosis (metaplasia) as a possible precursor; no established environmental, infectious, or hereditary cause.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; primary urethral clear-cell adenocarcinoma PMC3936309

name: Clear cell adenocarcinoma of the vagina
aliases: vaginal clear cell adenocarcinoma; diethylstilbestrol-associated vaginal adenocarcinoma
potential_causes: Classically caused by in utero exposure to diethylstilbestrol (DES), which produced vaginal adenosis as the precursor; non-DES sporadic cases also occur and have no established cause.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; Vaginal Adenocarcinoma review PMC12249244

name: Clear cell sarcoma of the kidney
potential_causes: Sporadic pediatric renal tumor with no established environmental, infectious, or hereditary cause; recurrent BCOR internal tandem duplication is a somatic driver, not an etiologic exposure.

name: Collecting duct carcinoma
potential_causes: Aggressive renal carcinoma arising from the collecting ducts of Bellini with no established environmental, infectious, or hereditary cause; it is sporadic and idiopathic.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; Pathology Outlines kidney collecting duct carcinoma

name: Ductal adenocarcinoma of the prostate
potential_causes: A prostatic adenocarcinoma variant arising from prostatic ducts; it shares the risk factors of acinar prostate cancer (age, family history, germline BRCA2 and other DNA repair gene mutations) with no distinct additional external cause.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; WHO 5th edition prostate cancer classification Kench 2022

name: ELOC-mutated renal cell carcinoma
aliases: elongin C-mutated renal cell carcinoma; TCEB1-mutated RCC
potential_causes: Sporadic renal cell carcinoma defined by biallelic somatic ELOC (TCEB1) inactivation; no established environmental, infectious, or hereditary cause, and the somatic mutation is not an etiologic exposure.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; WHO 2022 classification of renal neoplasms Cureus

name: Endometrioid carcinoma of the ovary
potential_causes: Strongly associated with endometriosis as a precursor lesion; a subset arises in Lynch syndrome (mismatch repair gene) carriers, and unopposed estrogen exposure is a contributing factor.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020

name: Eosinophilic solid and cystic renal cell carcinoma
potential_causes: Most cases are sporadic with somatic biallelic loss of TSC1 or TSC2, but a subset arises in patients with germline tuberous sclerosis complex, the established predisposing syndrome for these tumors.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; WHO 2022 classification of renal neoplasms Cureus

name: Epithelioid trophoblastic tumor
potential_causes: Arises from chorionic-type intermediate trophoblast of a prior gestation (term pregnancy, abortion, or molar pregnancy), which is the required predisposing event.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; gestational trophoblastic tumors review Arch Pathol Lab Med

name: Extramammary Paget disease of the vulva
potential_causes: Usually a primary intraepithelial adnexal (apocrine) neoplasm of unknown cause, though a minority is secondary to an underlying anorectal or urothelial adenocarcinoma; no established external etiologic factor.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; comprehensive management of vulvovaginal cancers PMC12432818

name: Fumarate hydratase-deficient renal cell carcinoma
aliases: FH-deficient RCC; hereditary leiomyomatosis and renal cell carcinoma-associated RCC
potential_causes: Caused by germline fumarate hydratase (FH) mutation defining hereditary leiomyomatosis and renal cell cancer (HLRCC) syndrome; the inherited FH defect is the predisposing cause.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; WHO 2022 classification of renal neoplasms Cureus

name: Gastric-type adenocarcinoma of the cervix
potential_causes: An HPV-independent cervical adenocarcinoma; a subset arises in Peutz-Jeghers syndrome (germline STK11 mutation), and lobular endocervical glandular hyperplasia is a recognized precursor lesion. Most cases are sporadic with somatic STK11 or TP53 alterations.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; gastric-type adenocarcinoma of the uterine cervix PMC11075765

name: Gestational choriocarcinoma
potential_causes: Arises from trophoblast of an antecedent pregnancy, most often following a complete hydatidiform mole but also after normal pregnancy, abortion, or ectopic pregnancy; a prior molar pregnancy is the principal predisposing event.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; gestational trophoblastic tumors review Arch Pathol Lab Med

name: High-grade endometrial stromal sarcoma
potential_causes: Sporadic uterine sarcoma defined by fusions such as YWHAE::NUTM2 or BCOR alterations; no established external, infectious, or hereditary etiologic factor.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; molecular classification of endometrial stromal sarcomas PMC7563240

name: High-grade serous carcinoma of the fallopian tube
potential_causes: Arises from serous tubal intraepithelial carcinoma (STIC) in the fallopian tube fimbria as the precursor lesion, with a strong hereditary component from germline BRCA1 and BRCA2 mutations (and other homologous recombination genes).
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; Pathology Outlines serous tubal intraepithelial carcinoma

name: Immature teratoma of the ovary
potential_causes: Sporadic germ cell tumor of young women with no established environmental, infectious, or hereditary cause.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; Diagnostic Histopathology ovarian germ cell tumours 2020

name: Invasive hydatidiform mole
potential_causes: A molar pregnancy (typically a complete hydatidiform mole from abnormal androgenetic fertilization) that invades the myometrium; it is by definition a complication of a preceding hydatidiform mole.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; gestational trophoblastic disease Dis Model Mech 2025

name: Juvenile granulosa cell tumor of the ovary
potential_causes: Mostly sporadic with no established cause; a small subset is associated with enchondromatosis (Ollier disease and Maffucci syndrome), and some cases carry AKT1 or GNAS alterations that are not etiologic exposures.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; Pathology Outlines ovarian sex cord stromal tumours

name: Large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma of the cervix
potential_causes: Caused by high-risk human papillomavirus infection (commonly HPV18 and HPV16), the same infectious etiology as other cervical carcinomas.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; MD Anderson small and large cell cervical cancer

name: Low-grade endometrial stromal sarcoma
potential_causes: Sporadic uterine mesenchymal tumor driven by recurrent gene fusions (commonly JAZF1::SUZ12); no established environmental, infectious, or hereditary cause, and prior pelvic radiation or tamoxifen are only weakly reported.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; JAZF1/SUZ12 gene fusion in endometrial stromal sarcomas PMC4754953

name: Low-grade serous carcinoma of the ovary
potential_causes: Arises through a stepwise pathway from serous borderline (atypical proliferative) tumors as a precursor lesion; established risk factors overlap with high-grade serous cancer, but no single external cause is defined and germline BRCA involvement is uncommon.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; Int J Gynecol Cancer rare ovarian tumors update 2024

name: Mesonephric adenocarcinoma of the cervix
potential_causes: Arises from embryonic mesonephric (Wolffian) duct remnants in the cervix as its cell of origin; HPV-independent, with no established environmental, infectious, or hereditary cause.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; histological classification of cervical adenocarcinomas Yasuda 2025

name: Mesonephric-like adenocarcinoma of the uterine corpus
potential_causes: Cause not established; thought to arise from Mullerian epithelium showing mesonephric differentiation (or from mesonephric remnants) driven by KRAS and PIK3CA mutations, with no known environmental, infectious, or hereditary etiologic factor.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; mesonephric-like adenocarcinoma of the uterine corpus PMC8625485

name: Micropapillary urothelial carcinoma
potential_causes: A urothelial carcinoma variant sharing established urothelial risk factors, principally cigarette smoking and occupational aromatic amine exposure.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; histologic variants of urothelial carcinoma Alderson 2020

name: Mucinous carcinoma of the ovary
potential_causes: Frequently arises through a benign to borderline to carcinoma sequence within mucinous cystadenomas and borderline tumors as precursor lesions; no established external cause, and smoking is a weak reported risk factor.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; Int J Gynecol Cancer rare ovarian tumors update 2024

name: Mucinous tubular and spindle cell carcinoma of the kidney
potential_causes: Sporadic low-grade renal carcinoma with no established environmental, infectious, or hereditary cause.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; mucinous tubular and spindle cell RCC review PMC4573286

name: Ovarian clear cell carcinoma
aliases: clear cell adenocarcinoma of the ovary
potential_causes: Strongly linked to endometriosis and endometriotic cysts as a precursor lesion, with additional risk from endometriosis-associated inflammation; a minority arise in Lynch syndrome (mismatch repair) carriers.

name: Ovarian dysgerminoma
potential_causes: Largely of unknown cause, but incidence is increased in dysgenetic gonads such as gonadal dysgenesis and androgen insensitivity with a Y chromosome (via gonadoblastoma as a precursor); most cases in normal ovaries are idiopathic.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; Diagnostic Histopathology ovarian germ cell tumours 2020

name: Ovarian yolk sac tumor
aliases: endodermal sinus tumor of the ovary
potential_causes: Sporadic malignant germ cell tumor with no established environmental, infectious, or hereditary cause in most cases.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; Pathology Outlines ovary yolk sac tumor

name: Penile squamous cell carcinoma
aliases: squamous cell carcinoma of the penis
potential_causes: About half are caused by high-risk human papillomavirus (chiefly HPV16); non-HPV cases are driven by chronic inflammation, phimosis, lichen sclerosus, poor hygiene, and smoking, with lack of circumcision as a risk factor.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; reclassification of penile SCC WHO 2022 PMC12231722

name: Placental site trophoblastic tumor
potential_causes: Arises from intermediate (implantation site) trophoblast of a prior gestation, typically months to years after a term pregnancy, non-molar abortion, or (less often) a hydatidiform mole; the antecedent pregnancy is the defining predisposing event.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; review of placental site trophoblastic tumors PMC6893905

name: Plasmacytoid urothelial carcinoma
potential_causes: An aggressive urothelial carcinoma variant defined by CDH1 (E-cadherin) loss; it shares urothelial carcinogen exposure, chiefly cigarette smoking, as the recognized risk factor.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; histologic variants of urothelial carcinoma Alderson 2020

name: Renal medullary carcinoma
potential_causes: Occurs almost exclusively in individuals with sickle cell trait or other sickle hemoglobinopathies (rarely sickle cell disease or hemoglobin SC), the defining predisposing condition.

name: Rhabdoid tumor of the kidney
aliases: malignant rhabdoid tumor of the kidney
potential_causes: Caused by biallelic inactivation of SMARCB1 (INI1), often with a germline mutation defining rhabdoid tumor predisposition syndrome; the inherited SWI/SNF defect is the recognized predisposing cause in a subset.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; Wilms Tumor and Other Childhood Kidney Tumors NCI PDQ NBK65953

name: Sarcomatoid urothelial carcinoma
potential_causes: A high-grade urothelial carcinoma variant sharing standard bladder cancer risk factors (cigarette smoking, occupational aromatic amine exposure); prior cyclophosphamide or pelvic radiation are additional recognized risks.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; recent advances in classification of bladder cancer Guo 2022

name: Sertoli-Leydig cell tumor of the ovary
potential_causes: A substantial subset arises in the DICER1 tumor predisposition syndrome (germline DICER1 mutation), the main established predisposing factor; sporadic cases have no other established cause.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; PMID sex cord stromal DICER1 FOXL2

name: Small cell carcinoma of the ovary hypercalcemic type
aliases: SCCOHT; malignant rhabdoid tumor of the ovary
potential_causes: Driven by germline or somatic inactivating mutations of SMARCA4 (BRG1), and germline carriers define a familial predisposition (rhabdoid tumor predisposition syndrome type 2); the underlying etiology beyond this SWI/SNF defect is otherwise unknown, with no established environmental, infectious, or hormonal cause.

name: Small cell neuroendocrine carcinoma of the cervix
aliases: SCNEC of the cervix; small cell cervical carcinoma
potential_causes: Caused by high-risk human papillomavirus, predominantly HPV18 (and HPV16), the same infectious etiology as other cervical carcinomas.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; small cell neuroendocrine carcinoma of the cervix UpToDate

name: Small cell neuroendocrine carcinoma of the prostate
aliases: small cell prostate carcinoma; neuroendocrine prostate carcinoma
potential_causes: Most cases arise as a treatment-emergent lineage transformation of prostatic adenocarcinoma under prolonged androgen deprivation and androgen receptor targeted therapy, the principal predisposing exposure; rare de novo cases have no established cause.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; neuroendocrine differentiation in prostate neoplasms PMC12695988

name: Small cell neuroendocrine carcinoma of the urinary bladder
aliases: small cell carcinoma of the bladder
potential_causes: Strongly associated with cigarette smoking, the same major carcinogen as urothelial carcinoma, and often coexists with urothelial carcinoma; it likely shares urothelial carcinogen exposures.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; recent advances in classification of bladder cancer Guo 2022

name: Spermatocytic tumor
potential_causes: A germ cell tumor of older men arising from spermatogonia with no established environmental, infectious, or hereditary cause; it is not associated with cryptorchidism or germ cell neoplasia in situ.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; WHO 5th edition classification of testicular tumours PMC9544657

name: Succinate dehydrogenase-deficient renal cell carcinoma
potential_causes: Caused by germline mutations in a succinate dehydrogenase subunit gene (most often SDHB), an inherited predisposition that also causes paraganglioma and pheochromocytoma; biallelic SDH loss defines the tumor.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; SDH-deficient RCC characterization PMID 25034258

name: Testicular Leydig cell tumor
aliases: Leydig cell tumour of the testis
potential_causes: Mostly sporadic sex cord-stromal tumor with no established external cause; rare cases occur in Klinefelter syndrome, and recurrent somatic activating mutations are not etiologic exposures.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; WHO 5th edition classification of testicular tumours PMC9544657

name: Testicular Sertoli cell tumor
aliases: Sertoli cell tumour of the testis
potential_causes: Mostly sporadic, but distinct subtypes arise in tumor predisposition syndromes: large cell calcifying Sertoli cell tumor in Carney complex (PRKAR1A) and intratubular large cell hyalinizing type in Peutz-Jeghers syndrome (STK11). Ordinary cases have no established cause.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; WHO 5th edition classification of testicular tumours PMC9544657

name: TFE3-rearranged renal cell carcinoma
aliases: Xp11 translocation renal cell carcinoma; MiT family translocation RCC
potential_causes: Some cases are associated with prior childhood cytotoxic chemotherapy exposure, a recognized predisposing factor; otherwise the tumor is sporadic and driven by TFE3 gene fusions that are not themselves an etiologic exposure.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; WHO 2022 classification of renal neoplasms Cureus

name: TFEB-altered renal cell carcinoma
aliases: TFEB-rearranged renal cell carcinoma; t(6;11) renal cell carcinoma
potential_causes: Sporadic MiT family translocation renal cell carcinoma driven by TFEB fusion or amplification; no established environmental, infectious, or hereditary etiologic factor.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; WHO 2022 classification of renal neoplasms Cureus

name: Tubulocystic renal cell carcinoma
potential_causes: Sporadic low-grade renal carcinoma with no established environmental, infectious, or hereditary cause.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; WHO 2022 classification of renal neoplasms Cureus

name: Urachal adenocarcinoma
potential_causes: Arises from persistent urachal remnants (the embryonic allantoic duct) at the bladder dome, with glandular or intestinal metaplasia as a precursor; no established environmental, infectious, or hereditary cause.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; recent advances in classification of bladder cancer Guo 2022

name: Urethral carcinoma
potential_causes: A subset is caused by high-risk human papillomavirus (chiefly HPV16), while other cases relate to chronic inflammation, urethral stricture, recurrent infection, and (in bulbar or proximal tumors) prior irritation; smoking is a contributing factor.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; clear cell adenocarcinoma of urethra PMC10789969

name: Uterine adenosarcoma
potential_causes: Mostly sporadic with no established cause; recognized risk factors include prior pelvic radiation, tamoxifen use, and long-term unopposed estrogen exposure.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; Uterine Sarcoma Treatment NCI PDQ

name: Uterine carcinosarcoma
potential_causes: Risk factors mirror endometrial carcinoma (obesity, unopposed estrogen exposure, nulliparity) and prior pelvic radiation and tamoxifen use are established predisposing exposures; it is regarded as a metaplastic (dedifferentiated) high-grade endometrial carcinoma.

name: Uterine clear cell carcinoma
potential_causes: A high-grade endometrial carcinoma of largely unknown cause; associations include prior pelvic radiation and tamoxifen use, but most cases are sporadic with no established external etiology.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; endometrial carcinoma changes to classification WHO 2020

name: Uterine leiomyosarcoma
potential_causes: Largely sporadic with no established cause; recognized risk factors include prior pelvic radiation and hereditary leiomyomatosis and renal cell cancer syndrome (germline fumarate hydratase), and tamoxifen exposure is a weaker association. Most cases arise de novo, not from benign leiomyomas.

name: Uterine serous carcinoma
aliases: endometrial serous carcinoma; uterine papillary serous carcinoma
potential_causes: A high-grade endometrial carcinoma usually arising in atrophic endometrium via serous endometrial intraepithelial carcinoma as the precursor; associated with older age, and a subset relates to germline TP53 (Li-Fraumeni) and tamoxifen or prior pelvic radiation exposure. Most cases have no single external cause.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; endometrial carcinoma changes to classification WHO 2020

name: Vaginal melanoma
potential_causes: A mucosal melanoma of largely unknown cause, not UV related; no established environmental, infectious, or hereditary etiologic factor.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; comprehensive management of vulvovaginal cancers PMC12432818

name: Vaginal squamous cell carcinoma
aliases: squamous cell carcinoma of the vagina
potential_causes: Most cases are caused by high-risk human papillomavirus (chiefly HPV16) through vaginal intraepithelial neoplasia as the precursor; additional risk from prior cervical HPV disease, smoking, and pelvic radiation.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; comprehensive management of vulvovaginal cancers PMC12432818

name: Vulvar melanoma
potential_causes: A mucosal or acral-type melanoma of largely unknown cause; unlike sun-exposed cutaneous melanoma it is not UV driven, and no established external cause is defined for most cases.
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; Cancer Research UK types of vulval cancer

name: Wilms tumor
potential_causes: Mostly sporadic, but a subset is driven by germline predisposition including WT1-related syndromes (WAGR, Denys-Drash), Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome (11p15 imprinting), and other overgrowth and DICER1 or Perlman syndromes; nephrogenic rests are the precursor lesion.

name: Acral lentiginous melanoma
potential_causes: Cause is largely unknown; it occurs on palms, soles, and nail units and is NOT strongly linked to UV exposure, showing low UV mutational burden, with mechanical trauma proposed but not established as a cause.

name: Adrenocortical carcinoma
potential_causes: Most cases are sporadic with no established cause; a minority arise in hereditary syndromes, notably Li-Fraumeni syndrome (germline TP53), Beckwith-Wiedemann and IGF2 overexpression (a major driver of pediatric ACC), and Lynch syndrome, with MEN1 and familial adenomatous polyposis as rarer predispositions.

name: Advanced basal cell carcinoma
aliases: metastatic basal cell carcinoma; locally advanced basal cell carcinoma
potential_causes: Driven overwhelmingly by chronic UV/sun exposure activating the Hedgehog pathway (PTCH1/SMO); additional causes include prior radiation, arsenic exposure, immunosuppression, and germline PTCH1 mutations in Gorlin (nevoid basal cell carcinoma) syndrome.
source: WHO Classification of Skin Tumours 5th ed (2023); Hedgehog pathway PTCH1/SMO (NEJM 2009 Von Hoff); metastasis rate <0.5%

name: Aggressive pituitary neuroendocrine tumor
potential_causes: Cause is largely unknown; most arise sporadically, with a minority linked to hereditary syndromes such as MEN1 (germline MEN1), Carney complex (PRKAR1A), and AIP-related familial isolated pituitary adenoma; no environmental cause is established.
source: WHO Classification of Endocrine and Neuroendocrine Tumours 5th ed; ~0.5% of adenomas display aggressive course (PMC7303064)

name: Anaplastic thyroid carcinoma
aliases: Anaplastic thyroid cancer; undifferentiated thyroid carcinoma; ATC
potential_causes: Frequently arises by dedifferentiation from a preexisting well-differentiated or poorly differentiated papillary or follicular thyroid carcinoma; long-standing goiter and prior radiation exposure are contributing risk factors, and TP53 plus BRAF/RAS mutations accumulate during progression.

name: Apocrine carcinoma of skin
potential_causes: Cause is unknown; this rare sweat gland malignancy arises most often in apocrine-rich areas (axilla, anogenital region) but has no established environmental, infectious, or hereditary etiology, and most cases are sporadic.
source: WHO Classification of Skin Tumours 5th ed (2023); Orphanet

name: Atypical fibroxanthoma
potential_causes: Arises on chronically sun-damaged skin of the head and neck in elderly patients and carries UV-signature TP53 mutations, implicating cumulative UV exposure; prior radiation and immunosuppression (organ transplant) are additional established risk factors.
source: WHO Classification of Skin Tumours 5th ed (2023); UV-induced TP53 mutations (PMID 32540221)

name: Carotid body paraganglioma
potential_causes: Caused both by germline SDHx mutations (classically SDHD in hereditary paraganglioma type 1, also SDHB/SDHC) and by chronic hypoxic stimulation of the carotid body, as in long-term high-altitude residence or chronic hypoxemic lung disease.
source: WHO Classification of Endocrine and Neuroendocrine Tumours 5th ed; Orphanet; SDHD germline (head and neck paraganglioma)

name: Conjunctival melanoma
potential_causes: Often arises from preexisting primary acquired melanosis (conjunctival melanocytic intraepithelial neoplasia) as a precursor lesion; UV/sun exposure is a proposed contributing factor, and it carries UV-signature and BRAF mutations in a subset.
source: WHO Classification; Orphanet; BRAF ~35% (PMID 27009410); incidence ~0.5 per million

name: Cutaneous angiosarcoma
potential_causes: Two well-established etiologic settings account for many cases: prior therapeutic radiation (for example post-breast-radiation chest wall angiosarcoma) and chronic lymphedema (Stewart-Treves syndrome); the remaining idiopathic scalp/face angiosarcomas of the elderly are linked to chronic sun exposure.
source: WHO Classification of Skin Tumours 5th ed (2023); Orphanet; MYC amplification in radiation/lymphedema-associated

name: Dermatofibrosarcoma protuberans
potential_causes: Defined by a somatic COL1A1-PDGFB gene fusion but has no established environmental or hereditary cause; the vast majority are sporadic, with prior trauma or scar reported only anecdotally and inconsistently.

name: Desmoplastic melanoma
potential_causes: Strongly linked to cumulative UV exposure, arising on chronically sun-damaged skin of the head and neck of older adults and carrying one of the highest UV mutational burdens of any melanoma subtype.
source: WHO Classification of Skin Tumours 5th ed (2023); NF1 in >50% (PMID 26076063); NFKBIE promoter (Nat Genet 2015)

name: Digital papillary adenocarcinoma
potential_causes: Cause is unknown; this aggressive sweat gland carcinoma characteristically arises on the fingers, palms, toes, and soles, but no environmental, occupational, or hereditary etiologic factor has been established and cases are sporadic.
source: WHO Classification of Skin Tumours 5th ed (2023); Orphanet; HPV42-associated (Leiendecker 2023 Cancer Discov)

name: Eccrine carcinoma
potential_causes: Cause is largely unknown; this heterogeneous group of malignant eccrine sweat gland tumors is mostly sporadic, with no consistently established environmental or hereditary cause, although radiation and immunosuppression are occasionally implicated.
source: WHO Classification of Skin Tumours 5th ed (2023); Orphanet

name: Endocrine mucin-producing sweat gland carcinoma
potential_causes: Cause is not established; this low-grade periocular sweat gland carcinoma, thought to arise from apocrine glands and considered a precursor of some primary cutaneous mucinous carcinomas, occurs sporadically in older adults with no identified risk factor.
source: WHO Classification of Skin Tumours 5th ed (2023); systematic review 110 cases (Abdulkader 2021 J Dermatolog Treat, PMID 34057875)

name: Extra-adrenal retroperitoneal paraganglioma
aliases: organ of Zuckerkandl paraganglioma; sympathetic paraganglioma
potential_causes: Strongly hereditary, most commonly driven by germline SDHx mutations (especially SDHB, which predisposes to extra-adrenal and malignant paragangliomas), along with VHL, NF1, and other susceptibility genes; the remainder are sporadic.
source: WHO Classification of Endocrine and Neuroendocrine Tumours 5th ed; SDHB confers highest metastatic risk (PMC10029328)

name: Extramammary Paget disease
potential_causes: Most cases are primary intraepithelial adenocarcinomas of unknown cause arising in apocrine-gland-rich skin (vulva, perianal, scrotal); a minority are secondary, representing epidermal spread from an underlying regional adenocarcinoma (for example anorectal or urothelial), which is the identifiable cause in those cases.
source: WHO Classification of Skin Tumours 5th ed (2023); Orphanet; HER2 in 30-60% (PMC7761697)

name: Hidradenocarcinoma
potential_causes: Can arise by malignant transformation of a preexisting benign hidradenoma (sweat gland tumor), but most cases have no established external cause and occur sporadically.
source: WHO Classification of Skin Tumours 5th ed (2023); Orphanet

name: Lentigo maligna melanoma
potential_causes: Directly caused by long-term cumulative UV/sun exposure, developing from a lentigo maligna (in situ) precursor on chronically sun-damaged skin of the face and head in older adults.
source: WHO Classification of Skin Tumours 5th ed (2023); chronic sun-damage (CSD) pathway

name: Malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumor
potential_causes: About half arise in patients with neurofibromatosis type 1 (germline NF1), typically via malignant transformation of a preexisting plexiform neurofibroma; prior therapeutic radiation is another established cause, and the remainder are sporadic.

name: Medullary thyroid carcinoma
potential_causes: About a quarter are hereditary, caused by germline activating mutations in the RET proto-oncogene in MEN2A, MEN2B, and familial MTC; the remaining sporadic cases carry somatic RET or RAS mutations and have no established environmental cause.

name: Melanoma arising in blue nevus
potential_causes: Arises by malignant transformation of a preexisting cellular blue nevus (the identifiable precursor lesion); it is driven by GNAQ/GNA11 mutations, is not UV-related, and has no established environmental cause otherwise.
source: WHO Classification of Skin Tumours 5th ed (2023); GNAQ/GNA11, BAP1 loss

name: Merkel cell carcinoma
potential_causes: Two established causal pathways: clonal integration of Merkel cell polyomavirus (about 80 percent of cases) and UV-induced DNA damage in virus-negative tumors; chronic immunosuppression (HIV, transplant, older age) is a strong additional risk factor.

name: Microcystic adnexal carcinoma
potential_causes: Cause is not firmly established, but the strongest associations are prior therapeutic or ionizing radiation to the site (often with decades of latency) and chronic UV/sun exposure of the head and neck; immunosuppression is a possible contributor.
source: WHO Classification of Skin Tumours 5th ed (2023); SEER incidence ~0.5 per million (Uptodate/Medscape)

name: Mucosal melanoma
potential_causes: Cause is largely unknown; unlike cutaneous melanoma it is NOT associated with UV exposure and lacks a UV mutational signature, arising sporadically from mucosal melanocytes of the head/neck, anogenital, and other sites.

name: Nevoid melanoma
potential_causes: Cause is not specifically established; this rare melanoma that histologically mimics a benign nevus occurs sporadically, and no distinct etiologic factor beyond the general melanoma risk factors (including UV exposure) has been defined for the subtype.
source: WHO Classification of Skin Tumours 5th ed (2023)

name: Paraganglioma
aliases: extra-adrenal paraganglioma; glomus tumor of head and neck
potential_causes: The most heritable of all human tumors, with germline mutations in SDHx (especially SDHD and SDHB), VHL, RET, NF1, MAX, and TMEM127; head and neck paragangliomas are additionally promoted by chronic hypoxia such as high-altitude residence.

name: Parathyroid carcinoma
potential_causes: Most cases are driven by inactivation of the CDC73 (HRPT2) gene, occurring somatically in sporadic tumors or as a germline mutation in hyperparathyroidism-jaw tumor syndrome; MEN1 and prior neck irradiation are additional predisposing factors.
source: WHO Classification of Endocrine and Neuroendocrine Tumours 5th ed; incidence ~3.5-5.7 per 10 million (SEER PMC8946517); CDC73/HRPT2

name: Pheochromocytoma
potential_causes: Up to 40 percent are driven by germline predisposition, most often mutations in SDHx (SDHB, SDHD, SDHC), VHL, RET (MEN2), NF1 (neurofibromatosis type 1), MAX, and TMEM127; the remainder are sporadic with no external environmental cause.

name: Pilomatrix carcinoma
potential_causes: Cause is largely unknown; the malignant counterpart of pilomatricoma, it is driven by activating CTNNB1 (beta-catenin) mutations but has no established environmental or hereditary etiologic factor, and most cases are sporadic.
source: WHO Classification of Skin Tumours 5th ed (2023); Orphanet

name: Pituitary carcinoma
aliases: metastatic pituitary neuroendocrine tumor; malignant pituitary tumor
potential_causes: Cause is largely unknown; these very rare tumors nearly always arise by malignant progression from a preexisting aggressive pituitary adenoma/neuroendocrine tumor rather than de novo, and no consistent environmental or hereditary etiology has been established (rare cases occur in MEN1 or AIP-related settings).
source: WHO Classification of Endocrine and Neuroendocrine Tumours 5th ed; ~0.2% of pituitary tumors (PMC9493437); defined by craniospinal/distant metastasis

name: Pleomorphic dermal sarcoma
potential_causes: Occurs on severely sun-damaged skin of the head and neck in elderly patients and harbors UV-induced TP53 driver mutations, implicating chronic UV exposure; prior radiation and immunosuppression are additional risk factors, and it is a more aggressive relative of atypical fibroxanthoma.
source: WHO Classification of Skin Tumours 5th ed (2023); recurrent UV-induced TP53, PIK3CA, RAS (PMID 32540221)

name: Poorly differentiated thyroid carcinoma
potential_causes: Typically arises by progression/dedifferentiation from a preexisting well-differentiated papillary or follicular thyroid carcinoma; prior radiation exposure and long-standing goiter are contributing risk factors, with RAS, TP53, and TERT promoter mutations accumulating during progression.
source: WHO Classification of Endocrine and Neuroendocrine Tumours 5th ed (Turin criteria); RAS/BRAF/TP53/CTNNB1 (PMC7286943)

name: Porocarcinoma
potential_causes: Frequently arises by malignant transformation of a preexisting benign poroma (eccrine sweat gland tumor); prior radiation, chronic sun exposure, and immunosuppression are reported risk factors, though many cases develop de novo.
source: WHO Classification of Skin Tumours 5th ed (2023); Orphanet; YAP1 fusions (Sekine 2019 J Clin Invest)

name: Primary cutaneous adenoid cystic carcinoma
potential_causes: Cause is unknown; this rare cutaneous adnexal carcinoma occurs sporadically, chiefly on the scalp and chest of adults, and no environmental, infectious, or hereditary etiologic factor has been established.
source: WHO Classification of Skin Tumours 5th ed (2023); Orphanet; MYB-NFIB fusion

name: Primary cutaneous cribriform carcinoma
potential_causes: Cause is unknown; consensus on etiology has not been reached for this rare low-grade cutaneous apocrine (cribriform) carcinoma, which arises sporadically on the extremities with no identified environmental or hereditary risk factor.
source: WHO Classification of Skin Tumours 5th ed (2023); downgraded to cribriform tumor in 5th ed

name: Primary cutaneous mucinous carcinoma
potential_causes: Cause is unknown; this rare low-grade sweat gland carcinoma (some cases evolving from endocrine mucin-producing sweat gland carcinoma) occurs sporadically, most often on the head and neck of older adults, with no established etiologic factor.
source: WHO Classification of Skin Tumours 5th ed (2023); incidence <0.1 per million (PathologyOutlines; PMC3481811)

name: Sebaceous carcinoma
aliases: sebaceous gland carcinoma; meibomian gland carcinoma
potential_causes: Most periocular cases are sporadic, but a significant subset arises in Muir-Torre syndrome, a variant of Lynch syndrome caused by germline mismatch repair mutations (MSH2, MLH1); UV exposure and prior radiation are additional risk factors.

name: Spiradenocarcinoma
potential_causes: Classically arises by malignant transformation of a long-standing benign spiradenoma or spiradenocylindroma, sometimes in the setting of Brooke-Spiegler syndrome (germline CYLD); otherwise no established environmental cause.
source: WHO Classification of Skin Tumours 5th ed (2023); Orphanet; arises in CYLD-mutant lesions

name: Spitz melanoma
